exercises.explain: Eyeballing images can tell you if they look bad, but our eyes are subjective, easily tired, and cannot measure precise mathematical improvements over thousands of images.
exercises.prompt: Why can't we rely only on humans looking at GAN images to decide if a model is getting better?

options.text: Our eyes get tired, we are subjective, and we cannot easily compare thousands of images.
Computers refuse to show us images if we do not use math tools.
Human eyes can only see images in low resolution.

cards.title: The Kitten Dilemma
cards.body: Imagine you've trained two different GANs. Both generate cute kittens. You look at 10 images from each, and they both look great! How do you decide which model is mathematically better? Relying purely on human eyes quickly becomes a bottleneck.

cards.title: Visual Inspection
cards.body: Visual inspection is the practice of checking generated images by eye. While useful for quick checks, it is subjective, does not scale to thousands of images, and cannot reliably compare different model versions.

Imagine trying to find the best chef in the world by taste-testing 10,000 dishes yourself. Your tastebuds would get tired, your opinions would shift, and you'd lose track. Instead, you need a standard scorecard.
cards.title: The Exhausted Food Critic

cards.body: Suppose Model A and Model B both generate 50,000 synthetic medical scans. A human radiologist might take weeks to look at just a fraction of them, and might change their mind on Tuesday compared to Monday. Quantitative metrics do this in seconds.
cards.title: Checking 50,000 Scans
Looks Good = Works Great?
cards.body: You might think, 'If it looks good to me, it's a good GAN!' But what if it only generates the same 3 good images over and over? Visual inspection misses issues like mode collapse and minor artifact patterns that humans overlook.
Let's compare relying on human eyes versus using standardized mathematical metrics.

right.text: Fast, objective, handles millions of images, gives clear numbers for comparison.

left.text: Slow, subjective, tires easily, struggles to compare model checkpoints fairly.

cards.title: Eyeballing vs. Measuring

exercises.explain: Quantitative metrics provide standard, mathematical, and objective scores that can be calculated automatically across thousands of images.
exercises.prompt: Why do AI researchers need quantitative metrics instead of just visual inspection?

options.text: They provide objective, automated, and repeatable measurements.
They make the images look prettier to the human eye.
They guarantee that the GAN will never fail during training.

exercises.explain: Visual inspection is subjective because different people have different opinions, and even the same person can change their mind depending on fatigue.
exercises.prompt: Visual inspection is highly subjective and can change based on the viewer's fatigue.
Complete the sentence: Visual inspection does not ___ because humans cannot look at millions of generated images to evaluate a model.
exercises.explain: Because we generate thousands of images to test GANs, manual visual inspection does not scale.
exercises.sentence: Visual inspection does not [blank] because humans cannot look at millions of generated images to evaluate a model.

exercises.explain: The first option shows a subjective human reaction, whereas the second option uses a quantitative, automated metric (Inception Score) to compare the models.

options.text: 'Model A's outputs look slightly more colorful to me than Model B's outputs.'
'Model A achieved an Inception Score of 8.2, while Model B achieved 7.5 over 10,000 images.'

exercises.prompt: Which of these represents a quantitative evaluation rather than a visual inspection?
What is the main danger of relying only on 'looking' at GAN outputs to judge training progress?

options.text: We cannot reliably prove progress or choose the best model version.
The GAN will stop training immediately.
The generator will start outputting text instead of images.

exercises.explain: Without math-based metrics, we have no objective yardstick to prove one training run is better than another. Next, we will learn about the specific math metrics used to measure GAN quality.

concept: Visual inspection is subjective, does not scale, and cannot easily compare different models or training runs, making quantitative metrics necessary.
title: Why 'Just Look at It' Isn't Enough
